questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black Tree depicted below, which case will be invoked when we insert node 65 into the tree?

Note 1: In the diagram, black nodes have a black background and red nodes have a red background.
Note 2: Remember to consider NIL nodes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    40((40)) --- 20((20))
    40 --- 60((60))
    60 --- 50((50))
    60 --- 80((80))
    80 --- 70((70))
    80 --- NIL[NIL]
    style 20 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 40 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 60 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 50 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 80 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 70 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style NIL fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```
questions.explanation: Node 65 is inserted as the left child of node 70 because 65 < 70. This results in a red-red violation between 70 and 65. The uncle of the newly inserted node 65 is the right child of node 80, which is a black NIL node. Since the path from the grandparent (80) through the parent (70) to the node (65) is linear (left-child of a left-child), this triggers Case 3 (rotation and recoloring) in the standard Red-Black Tree insertion algorithm.

questions.text:
Based on the same Red-Black Tree structure, which case will be invoked when we insert node 10 into the tree?

```mermaid
graph TD
    40((40)) --- 20((20))
    40 --- 60((60))
    60 --- 50((50))
    60 --- 80((80))
    80 --- 70((70))
    80 --- NIL[NIL]
    style 20 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 40 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 60 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 50 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 80 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 70 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style NIL fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```
questions.explanation: Node 10 will be inserted as the left child of node 20. Because node 20 is black, no red-black tree properties are violated when a red node is added as its child. Therefore, no fixing cases are invoked.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black Tree depicted below, what is the height of the tree?

Note: Height is defined as the number of edges on the longest path from the root to a leaf node.

```mermaid
graph TD
    40((40)) --- 20((20))
    40 --- 60((60))
    60 --- 50((50))
    60 --- 80((80))
    80 --- 70((70))
    80 --- NIL[NIL]
    style 20 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 40 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 60 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 50 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 80 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 70 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style NIL fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```
questions.explanation: The height is the number of edges on the longest path from the root. The longest path is 40 \(\rightarrow\) 60 \(\rightarrow\) 80 \(\rightarrow\) 70, which consists of 3 edges.

questions.text: The array \(\{40, 35, 30, 15, 37, 25, 20, 10, 12\}\) represents a complete binary tree but is not a max-heap. Only one pair of nodes violates the max-heap property. Which pair (parent and child) is it?
questions.explanation: Using 1-based indexing for the heap, the parent of an element at index \(j\) is at index \(\lfloor j/2 \rfloor\). At index 2 (value 35), the children are at index 4 (value 15) and index 5 (value 37). In a max-heap, the parent must be greater than or equal to its children. Since 35 < 37, the property is violated by the pair (35, 37).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black Tree depicted below, list the keys of the nodes using a pre-order traversal (Root, Left, Right).

```mermaid
graph TD
    40((40)) --- 20((20))
    40 --- 60((60))
    60 --- 50((50))
    60 --- 80((80))
    80 --- 70((70))
    80 --- NIL[NIL]
    style 20 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 40 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 60 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 50 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 80 fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style 70 fill:#f00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style NIL fill:#000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```
questions.explanation: A pre-order traversal visits the root first, then the left subtree, and then the right subtree. Following this: visit 40, then visit its left child 20. Then visit the right subtree starting at 60. From 60, visit its left child 50, then its right child 80, and finally 80's left child 70. This yields: 40, 20, 60, 50, 80, 70.

questions.text: If \(f(n) = 5\) and \(g(n) = e^{10}\), which of the following asymptotic relationships are true?
questions.explanation: Both \(f(n)\) and \(g(n)\) are constant functions (they do not grow with \(n\)). All positive constant functions belong to the same complexity class, \(\Theta(1)\). Therefore, \(f(n)\) is simultaneously \(O(g(n))\), \(\Omega(g(n))\), and \(\Theta(g(n))\).

questions.text: Given the array \(A = \langle 15, 2, 8, 1, 10, 5, 12, 7 \rangle\), what is the resulting array after applying the partition procedure in the Quicksort algorithm once (using the last element as the pivot)?
questions.explanation: Using Lomuto partition with pivot 7: (1) 15 > 7, (2) 2 <= 7: swap 15 and 2, (3) 8 > 7, (4) 1 <= 7: swap 15 and 1, (5) 10 > 7, (6) 5 <= 7: swap 8 and 5, (7) 12 > 7. Finally, swap the pivot (7) with the element at the index following the last element smaller than the pivot (15). The array becomes: 2, 1, 5, 7, 10, 8, 12, 15.

questions.text: Given an array \(A = [10, 5, 20, 15, 30, 40, 50]\), what will the array be after applying the Max-Heapify procedure at index 2 (the node with value 5)?
questions.explanation: Max-Heapify at index 2 (value 5) compares the parent with its children at indices 4 (value 15) and 5 (value 30). The largest is 30. We swap 5 and 30. The array becomes [10, 30, 20, 15, 5, 40, 50]. Since index 5 is a leaf node, the recursion stops.

questions.text: In a Red-Black Tree, what is the relationship between the height \(h(x)\) and the black-height \(bh(x)\) of any node \(x\)?
questions.explanation: By the properties of Red-Black Trees, at least half of the nodes on any simple path from a node to a leaf (excluding the node itself) must be black. Therefore, the total height is at most twice the black-height: \(h \le 2bh\).

questions.text: Using the Master Theorem, what is the solution to the recurrence relation \(T(n) = 4T(n / 4) + \log n\)?
questions.explanation: Here, \(a=4, b=4\), and \(f(n) = \log n\). We compare \(f(n)\) with \(n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_4 4} = n^1\). Since \(f(n) = \log n = O(n^{1-\epsilon})\) for some \(\epsilon > 0\) (Case 1 of the Master Theorem), the solution is \(T(n) = \Theta(n)\).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree described below, what is the inorder successor of node 60?

**Structure:**
- Node 40 (Black) is the root.
- Node 40 has a right child 60 (Red).
- Node 60 has a left child 50 (Black) and a right child 80 (Black).
- Node 80 has a left child 70 (Red).
- All leaves are NIL (Black) nodes.
questions.explanation: In a binary search tree, the inorder successor of a node with a right subtree is the leftmost node in that right subtree. The right child of node 60 is 80, and the leftmost node in the subtree rooted at 80 is 70.

questions.text: Given a binary heap that contains 512 nodes, what is the height of the sub-heap rooted at level 4? (Assume the root of the entire heap is at level 0).
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree with \(N = 512\) nodes has a height of \(H = \lfloor \log_2 512 \rfloor = 9\). If the root is at level 0, the maximum level in the heap is 9. The height of a node at level 4 is the distance to the deepest level: \(9 - 4 = 5\).

The outer loop in `main` runs \(n\) times. For each iteration \(i\), the function `CALC(i)` is called. If \(i \ge 10\), `CALC` performs a loop that runs \(i/2\) times. The total number of operations is proportional to \(\sum_{i=10}^{n-1} i/2\), which is an arithmetic series resulting in \(O(n^2)\).

questions.text: Which traversal method outputs the data of a Binary Search Tree (BST) in non-decreasing sorted order?
questions.explanation: Inorder traversal visits a node's left subtree, then the node itself, then its right subtree. For any node in a BST, all values in the left subtree are smaller and all values in the right subtree are larger, so this traversal produces sorted output.

questions.text: Given the recurrence relation \( T(n) = n^2 + 3 T(n / 2) \), how many nodes are present at level \(i = 4\) of the recursion tree?
questions.explanation: In the recursion tree for \( T(n) = a T(n/b) + f(n) \), the number of nodes at level \(i\) is \(a^i\). Here, \(a=3\) and \(i=4\). Therefore, the number of nodes is \(3^4 = 81\).

questions.text: A complete binary search tree with \(n\) nodes has a worst-case height of:
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree is balanced by definition. Its height is always logarithmic relative to the number of nodes, specifically \(\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor\).

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree described below, what is the black-height (bh) of node 60?

**Structure:**
- Node 40 (Black) is the root.
- Node 40 has a right child 60 (Red).
- Node 60 has a left child 50 (Black) and a right child 80 (Black).
- Node 80 has a left child 70 (Red).
- All leaves are NIL (Black) nodes.
questions.explanation: The black-height \(bh(x)\) of a node is the number of black nodes on any path from \(x\) to a leaf, not counting \(x\) itself but counting the NIL leaves. From node 60, a path to a leaf is 60 -> 50 (Black) -> NIL (Black). There are 2 black nodes on this path (50 and NIL).

questions.text: Insert the following elements into an initially empty binary max-heap: \( \langle 5, 10, 3, 8, 1, 12, 7 \rangle \). After all insertions are complete, what data element is the left child of the root?
questions.explanation:
Step-by-step insertion into the max-heap:
1. [5]
2. [10, 5]
3. [10, 5, 3]
4. [10, 8, 3, 5]
5. [10, 8, 3, 5, 1]
6. [12, 8, 10, 5, 1, 3]
7. [12, 8, 10, 5, 1, 3, 7]
The root is 12. Its left child (index 1 in a 0-indexed array) is 8.

questions.text: Given the array \( A = \{15, 5, 20, 10, 2\} \), what will the array look like after the outer loop of the insertion sort algorithm has completed three iterations?

Based on the Red-Black tree described below, what is the depth of node 40?

**Structure:**
- Node 40 (Black) is the root.
- Node 40 has a right child 60 (Red).
- Node 60 has a left child 50 (Black) and a right child 80 (Black).
- Node 80 has a left child 70 (Red).
- All leaves are NIL (Black) nodes.
questions.explanation: Node 40 is the root of the tree. By definition, the depth of the root node is 0.

questions.text:
Based on the Red-Black tree depicted below, answer the following questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
  node100((100))
  node50((50))
  node150((150))
  node25((25))
  node75((75))
  node10((10))
  node100 --> node50
  node100 --> node150
  node50 --> node25
  node50 --> node75
  node25 --> node10
  style node100 fill:#000,color:#fff
  style node150 fill:#000,color:#fff
  style node50 fill:#fff,stroke:#f00,color:#f00
  style node25 fill:#000,color:#fff
  style node75 fill:#000,color:#fff
  style node10 fill:#fff,stroke:#f00,color:#f00
```

Which case will be invoked when we insert node 5 into the tree?
questions.explanation: Node 5 is inserted as the left child of 10. Node 10 is red, creating a red-red violation. The uncle of 10 is the right child of 25 (which is NIL/Black). Since the relationship is a straight line (Left-Left), Case 3 (Rotate and Recolor) is invoked.

questions.text: Based on the Red-Black tree provided in the first question, which case will be invoked when we insert node 200 into the tree?
questions.explanation: Node 200 is inserted as the right child of node 150. Since node 150 is black, no properties of the Red-Black tree are violated, and no rebalancing cases are invoked.

questions.text: Based on the Red-Black tree provided in the first question, what is the height of the tree?
questions.explanation: Height is defined as the number of edges on the longest path from the root to a leaf. The longest path is 100 -> 50 -> 25 -> 10, which consists of 3 edges.

questions.text: List the keys of the nodes in the Red-Black tree from the first question using pre-order traversal.
questions.explanation: Pre-order traversal follows the Root -> Left -> Right sequence: 100 (Root), 50 (Left child of 100), 25 (Left child of 50), 10 (Left child of 25), 75 (Right child of 50), 150 (Right child of 100).

questions.text: What is the successor of node 50 in the Red-Black tree from the first question?
questions.explanation: The in-order successor is the smallest key greater than the current key. In a BST, if the node has a right child, the successor is the leftmost node in its right subtree. For node 50, the right child is 75, and 75 has no left subtree; thus, 75 is the successor.

questions.text: What is the black height (bh) of node 50 in the Red-Black tree from the first question?
questions.explanation: Black height is the number of black nodes on any path from the node to a leaf (excluding the node itself). From node 50: path to NIL via 75 involves node 75(B) and NIL(B), total 2. Path via 25 involves 25(B), 10(R), and NIL(B), total 2.

questions.text: What is the depth of node 10 in the Red-Black tree from the first question?
questions.explanation: Depth is the number of edges from the root to the node. Path from root 100 to 10: 100 -> 50, 50 -> 25, 25 -> 10. Total 3 edges.

questions.text: Given the functions \( f(n) = 10 \) and \( g(n) = 1000 \), which of the following is true?
questions.explanation: Both \( f(n) \) and \( g(n) \) are constant functions. Asymptotically, all constant functions belong to the same complexity class, meaning they are \( O \), \( \Omega \), and \( \Theta \) of each other.

questions.text: Given the array A = \( \langle 8, 3, 9, 1, 5 \rangle \), what is the resulting array after applying the Quick Sort partition procedure once, using the last element (5) as the pivot?
questions.explanation: Using Lomuto partition with pivot 5: 8 is greater than 5; 3 is less than 5, so swap 8 and 3 ([3, 8, 9, 1, 5]); 9 is greater than 5; 1 is less than 5, so swap 8 and 1 ([3, 1, 9, 8, 5]). Finally, swap the pivot (5) with the first element larger than it (9), resulting in [3, 1, 5, 8, 9].

questions.text: Let \( T(n) \) be a function obeying the recurrence \( T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \). Which of the following describes \( T(n) \)?
questions.explanation: Using the Master Theorem: \( a=2, b=2, f(n)=n \). Since \( n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 \), we have \( f(n) = \Theta(n) \). This corresponds to Case 2, where \( T(n) = \Theta(n \log n) \). Therefore, \( T(n) = O(n \log n) \) is also correct.
